EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm combined with an atmospheric river brought heavy rain and snow to southern California. 3 day precipitation amounts as high as 4 to 7 inches occurred in the San Bernardino Mountains. 1 hour rainfall mountains of 0.5 to 0.9 inches per hour were observed in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino/San Gabriel mountains. Total rainfall of 6 inches and a maximum one hour rate of 0.65 inches during the evening of February 13, which produced a significant debris flow and flash flood on SR 330 which trapped 3 vehicles. The damage on the road resulted in a 2 week closure to the state highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station DVOC1 Devore reported a 2-day rainfall of 4.18 inches.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the system which brought generally light precipitation from Fresno County northward on February 1, unseasonably warm temperatures prevailed across the area on February 2 with record highs being approached at several locations. A strong system moved into northern California during the morning of February 3 which slowly pushed southward into central California on February 4 and brought precipitation to the area between the afternoon of February 4 and the morning of February 5. Precipitation totals were less than anticipated although much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon picked up 1 to 2 inches of liquid precipitation with a half inch to 1.25 inches from Kings Canyon southward. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and three tenths of an inch of rain. The Tehachapi Mountains generally had a tenth of an inch of rain or less while the Kern County Deserts remained dry, but had a period of strong wind gusts during the afternoon and evening of February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph. A few stations had gusts exceeding 60 mph ahead of the arrival of the precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system and associated trough moved across California on February 13 bringing widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area along with damaging wind gusts. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported on February 13. There were also several reports of trees and power lines knocked down by strong wind gusts as several station reported gusts above 60 mph and a few stations had gusts near 90 mph. Much of the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 4 and 6 inches of liquid precipitation while the Sierra Foothills generally had 2.5 to 5 inches of rainfall. The Kern River Valley generally picked up between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall while much of the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills from Fresno County northward picked up between 1 and 2 inches. South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alcade RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain with river and roadway flooding, and gusty southerly winds February 3rd through 5th. Multiple feet of heavy snow were observed above 5000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of rain observed in the Valley and foothills and southerly wind gusts of up to 50 mph. There were numerous reports of flooding in the Valley that led to multiple search and rescue efforts and prolonged road closures. Portions of Highway 99 were closed due to flooding, State Route 70 was closed from Greenville Wye to Jarbo Gap due to a slide, and Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to crashes and icy roads. There were also reports of numerous downed branches and trees across the area with associated power outages. Glenn County reported over 20 road closures across their area due to flooding and storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans District 3 measured 15 to 20 inches of snow along Interstate 80 from February 3rd through February 5th above 5500 feet. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 25 inches of heavy snow over the same timeframe. Ski resorts in the area measured 1 to 3 feet of snow. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 1 to 3 feet of snow above 5500 feet with 4 feet at the higher peaks.  Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to a crash and icy roads.

South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Inyokern Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain with river and roadway flooding, and gusty southerly winds February 3rd through 5th. Multiple feet of heavy snow were observed above 5000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of rain observed in the Valley and foothills and southerly wind gusts of up to 50 mph. There were numerous reports of flooding in the Valley that led to multiple search and rescue efforts and prolonged road closures. Portions of Highway 99 were closed due to flooding, State Route 70 was closed from Greenville Wye to Jarbo Gap due to a slide, and Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to crashes and icy roads. There were also reports of numerous downed branches and trees across the area with associated power outages. Glenn County reported over 20 road closures across their area due to flooding and storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding from heavy rain at State Route 36 and Monroe Avenue near Red Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm from late February 12th into Friday, February 14th brought heavy snow, rain, and gusty winds. This brought from 1 to 6 inches of rain, with totals of 2 to nearly 4 feet of heavy snow above 4500 feet. Gusty southerly wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph in the Valley, 40 to 60 mph in the mountains and foothills were recorded. Roads were reported to be flooded, as well as covered with mud and rocks in some mountain and foothill areas. Numerous downed trees were reported during this time, along with mountain highway closures and chain restrictions, with roadway flooding in the Central Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reporting eastbound lanes flooded at SR-4 and Stallion Way from Heavy Rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm system brought another round of heavy snow, rain, rock slides and gusty winds from February 6th through February 7th. Generally 4 to 12 inches were observed above 3000 feet in Shasta County and the Coast Range and above 5000 feet in the Sierra and Southern Cascades, with up to 18 inches at the higher peaks. Interstate 5 closed for about 4 hours at Pollard Flat due to heavy snow. Traffic cameras showed light snowfall reported down to 800 feet in Shasta County as well. State Route 70 closed from Jarbo Gap to Greenville Wye due to a significant rock slide. California Highway Patrol reported multiple cars stuck in snow in the mountains and a few downed trees in the Valley and foothills. There were multiple reports of roadway flooding in the Valley and foothills, including the closure of Highway 99 between Garner Lane and Keefer Road due to floodwaters covering the roadway. The Sacramento River experienced flooding due to heavy rain and high flows that led to the flooding of two RV Parks within the levee along the river in Colusa and Sutter Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby CoCoRaHS at similar elevations reported 5 to 7 inches of snow above 3000 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated snow amounts of 4 to 12 inches above 3000 feet with up to 18 inches at the higher peaks. Interstate 5 was closed at Pollard Flat for about 4 hours Thursday afternoon through evening due to heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The latest in this series of winter storms impacted the Sierra Feb 3rd through the 5th. This system had lower snow levels, continued strong winds, and flooding concerns across northwest Lassen County. Forecast guidance showed a strong winter storm lasting to the night of 4th until the morning of the 5th. Threats with this storm include strong winds to 60 mph on some valley floors of western NV with blowing dust possible in some basin and Range locations and choppy lake conditions. Heavy mountain snow brought impacts over mountain passes, and valley rain allowed for some possible flooding in portions of Plumas and Sierra counties in addition to the minor flood impacts along the Susan River at Susanville, California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at the Palisades Tahoe Ski Base, a mesonet station (HVRGM-Blue Streak Weather Station) located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski summit, and ASOS ( KTVL - South Lake Tahoe Airport) located 3.0 mi N of Meyers, CA  reported a peak wind gust 145 mph, 96 mph, and 64 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm combined with an atmospheric river brought heavy rain and snow to southern California. 3 day precipitation amounts as high as 4 to 7 inches occurred in the San Bernardino Mountains. 1 hour rainfall mountains of 0.5 to 0.9 inches per hour were observed in the Santa Ana and San Bernardino/San Gabriel mountains. Total rainfall of 6 inches and a maximum one hour rate of 0.65 inches during the evening of February 13, which produced a significant debris flow and flash flood on SR 330 which trapped 3 vehicles. The damage on the road resulted in a 2 week closure to the state highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a report of a flooded roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the system which brought generally light precipitation from Fresno County northward on February 1, unseasonably warm temperatures prevailed across the area on February 2 with record highs being approached at several locations. A strong system moved into northern California during the morning of February 3 which slowly pushed southward into central California on February 4 and brought precipitation to the area between the afternoon of February 4 and the morning of February 5. Precipitation totals were less than anticipated although much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon picked up 1 to 2 inches of liquid precipitation with a half inch to 1.25 inches from Kings Canyon southward. Most of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and three tenths of an inch of rain. The Tehachapi Mountains generally had a tenth of an inch of rain or less while the Kern County Deserts remained dry, but had a period of strong wind gusts during the afternoon and evening of February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 45 mph. A few stations had gusts exceeding 60 mph ahead of the arrival of the precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 3 to 6 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the area during the evening of February 5. With clear skies and light winds prevailing across the central portion of the San Joaquin Valley, some patches of dense fog formed toward sunrise on February 6. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the area during the evening of February 5. With clear skies and light winds prevailing across the central portion of the San Joaquin Valley, some patches of dense fog formed toward sunrise on February 6. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system and associated trough moved across California on February 13 bringing widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area along with damaging wind gusts. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported on February 13. There were also several reports of trees and power lines knocked down by strong wind gusts as several station reported gusts above 60 mph and a few stations had gusts near 90 mph. Much of the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 4 and 6 inches of liquid precipitation while the Sierra Foothills generally had 2.5 to 5 inches of rainfall. The Kern River Valley generally picked up between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall while much of the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills from Fresno County northward picked up between 1 and 2 inches. South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Pigeon Spring measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved inland out of the Pacific Ocean into central California during the morning of February 6. As a result, widespread precipitation spread across the area during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of liquid precipitation with the snow level rising from 6500 feet on the morning of February 6 to around 8000 feet by late afternoon. Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. Much of the Sierra Foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall while much of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills north of Kern County as well as the Kern River Valley generally picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rain. Rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley,  the Tehachapi Mountains and over the Kern County Desert areas. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the area with several ridge top stations and stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph while a few stations had gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alcalde RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system and associated trough moved across California on February 13 bringing widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area along with damaging wind gusts. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported on February 13. There were also several reports of trees and power lines knocked down by strong wind gusts as several station reported gusts above 60 mph and a few stations had gusts near 90 mph. Much of the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 4 and 6 inches of liquid precipitation while the Sierra Foothills generally had 2.5 to 5 inches of rainfall. The Kern River Valley generally picked up between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall while much of the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills from Fresno County northward picked up between 1 and 2 inches. South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense atmospheric river impacted the Bay Area from February 3rd to the 4th, bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the region, especially across the North Bay. The Russian River near Guerneville flooded due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was found dead around 7 a.m. On February 6th at Sanford and Hall roads, a rural stretch west of Santa Rosa lined with scattered homes and open fields. Sheriff���s deputies had been searching for an 83-year-old man reported missing around 9 p.m. on the 5th. Investigators have not yet confirmed whether the recovered body is his.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain with river and roadway flooding, and gusty southerly winds February 3rd through 5th. Multiple feet of heavy snow were observed above 5000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of rain observed in the Valley and foothills and southerly wind gusts of up to 50 mph. There were numerous reports of flooding in the Valley that led to multiple search and rescue efforts and prolonged road closures. Portions of Highway 99 were closed due to flooding, State Route 70 was closed from Greenville Wye to Jarbo Gap due to a slide, and Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to crashes and icy roads. There were also reports of numerous downed branches and trees across the area with associated power outages. Glenn County reported over 20 road closures across their area due to flooding and storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the closure of State Route 99 due to flooding between Keefer Road and Esplanade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm system brought another round of heavy snow, rain, rock slides and gusty winds from February 6th through February 7th. Generally 4 to 12 inches were observed above 3000 feet in Shasta County and the Coast Range and above 5000 feet in the Sierra and Southern Cascades, with up to 18 inches at the higher peaks. Interstate 5 closed for about 4 hours at Pollard Flat due to heavy snow. Traffic cameras showed light snowfall reported down to 800 feet in Shasta County as well. State Route 70 closed from Jarbo Gap to Greenville Wye due to a significant rock slide. California Highway Patrol reported multiple cars stuck in snow in the mountains and a few downed trees in the Valley and foothills. There were multiple reports of roadway flooding in the Valley and foothills, including the closure of Highway 99 between Garner Lane and Keefer Road due to floodwaters covering the roadway. The Sacramento River experienced flooding due to heavy rain and high flows that led to the flooding of two RV Parks within the levee along the river in Colusa and Sutter Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two CoCoRaHS in Shingletown measured 5.8 inches and 6.4 inches of heavy snow above 3000 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 6 to 12 inches above 5000 feet with up to 18 inches at Lassen Peak.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly man went missing while walking his dog in Garland Ranch Regional Park, and was last heard from around 10:30 AM. Around 11 AM, hikers found his dog and returned it to the man's family, who reported him missing at noon. Monterey County park rangers were assisted by local and state resources in a swift-water search, who ultimately found the man's body in Carmel River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The latest in this series of winter storms impacted the Sierra Feb 3rd through the 5th. This system had lower snow levels, continued strong winds, and flooding concerns across northwest Lassen County. Forecast guidance showed a strong winter storm lasting to the night of 4th until the morning of the 5th. Threats with this storm include strong winds to 60 mph on some valley floors of western NV with blowing dust possible in some basin and Range locations and choppy lake conditions. Heavy mountain snow brought impacts over mountain passes, and valley rain allowed for some possible flooding in portions of Plumas and Sierra counties in addition to the minor flood impacts along the Susan River at Susanville, California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP Observer station (TAHC1) located at Tahoe City, CA, reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 1.39 inches.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved inland out of the Pacific Ocean into central California during the morning of February 6. As a result, widespread precipitation spread across the area during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of liquid precipitation with the snow level rising from 6500 feet on the morning of February 6 to around 8000 feet by late afternoon. Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. Much of the Sierra Foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall while much of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills north of Kern County as well as the Kern River Valley generally picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rain. Rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley,  the Tehachapi Mountains and over the Kern County Desert areas. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the area with several ridge top stations and stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph while a few stations had gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crane Flat RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm system brought another round of heavy snow, rain, rock slides and gusty winds from February 6th through February 7th. Generally 4 to 12 inches were observed above 3000 feet in Shasta County and the Coast Range and above 5000 feet in the Sierra and Southern Cascades, with up to 18 inches at the higher peaks. Interstate 5 closed for about 4 hours at Pollard Flat due to heavy snow. Traffic cameras showed light snowfall reported down to 800 feet in Shasta County as well. State Route 70 closed from Jarbo Gap to Greenville Wye due to a significant rock slide. California Highway Patrol reported multiple cars stuck in snow in the mountains and a few downed trees in the Valley and foothills. There were multiple reports of roadway flooding in the Valley and foothills, including the closure of Highway 99 between Garner Lane and Keefer Road due to floodwaters covering the roadway. The Sacramento River experienced flooding due to heavy rain and high flows that led to the flooding of two RV Parks within the levee along the river in Colusa and Sutter Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at State Route 99 north and Broadway offramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure on an upper trough on February 8 that brought widespread precipitation to the area during the previous two days, a cold and dry airmass settled over the area and resulted is widespread sub-freezing minimum temperatures in the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County on February 9. Upslope clouds kept temperatures above the freezing mark in the much of the center portion of the valley the following two mornings although although most of Merced County and western Fresno County had sub-freezing minimum temperatures on February 10 and February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees each day between February 9 and February 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved inland out of the Pacific Ocean into central California during the morning of February 6. As a result, widespread precipitation spread across the area during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of liquid precipitation with the snow level rising from 6500 feet on the morning of February 6 to around 8000 feet by late afternoon. Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. Much of the Sierra Foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall while much of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills north of Kern County as well as the Kern River Valley generally picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rain. Rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley,  the Tehachapi Mountains and over the Kern County Desert areas. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the area with several ridge top stations and stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph while a few stations had gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of N. Friant Rd. and North Fork Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system and associated trough moved across California on February 13 bringing widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area along with damaging wind gusts. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported on February 13. There were also several reports of trees and power lines knocked down by strong wind gusts as several station reported gusts above 60 mph and a few stations had gusts near 90 mph. Much of the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 4 and 6 inches of liquid precipitation while the Sierra Foothills generally had 2.5 to 5 inches of rainfall. The Kern River Valley generally picked up between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall while much of the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills from Fresno County northward picked up between 1 and 2 inches. South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Grapevine measured a peak wind gust of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge shifted inland from offshore into California during the morning of February 21. This resulted in light winds and clearing skies in the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley which allowed for some patches of dense fog to develop. While the fog was short lived in most locations, it persisted in Hanford until well after sunrise before lifting into a low cloud deck before dissipating by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain with river and roadway flooding, and gusty southerly winds February 3rd through 5th. Multiple feet of heavy snow were observed above 5000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of rain observed in the Valley and foothills and southerly wind gusts of up to 50 mph. There were numerous reports of flooding in the Valley that led to multiple search and rescue efforts and prolonged road closures. Portions of Highway 99 were closed due to flooding, State Route 70 was closed from Greenville Wye to Jarbo Gap due to a slide, and Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to crashes and icy roads. There were also reports of numerous downed branches and trees across the area with associated power outages. Glenn County reported over 20 road closures across their area due to flooding and storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sacramento County Sheriff reported roadway flooding from Deer Creek overflow on Scott Road leading to the closure of Scott Road between Boys Ranch Road and Latrobe Road.

Rainfall amounts were generally a tenth of an inch or less in the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley,  the Tehachapi Mountains and over the Kern County Desert areas. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the area with several ridge top stations and stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph while a few stations had gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved inland out of the Pacific Ocean into central California during the morning of February 6. As a result, widespread precipitation spread across the area during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.75 and 1.50 inches of liquid precipitation with the snow level rising from 6500 feet on the morning of February 6 to around 8000 feet by late afternoon. Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall. Much of the Sierra Foothills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall while much of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills north of Kern County as well as the Kern River Valley generally picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rain. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the area with several ridge top stations and stations in the Sierra Nevada measuring gusts exceeding 45 mph while a few stations had gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs above 8000 feet picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system and associated trough moved across California on February 13 bringing widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area along with damaging wind gusts. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported on February 13. There were also several reports of trees and power lines knocked down by strong wind gusts as several station reported gusts above 60 mph and a few stations had gusts near 90 mph. Much of the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 4 and 6 inches of liquid precipitation while the Sierra Foothills generally had 2.5 to 5 inches of rainfall. The Kern River Valley generally picked up between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall while much of the Tehachapi Mountains and the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills from Fresno County northward picked up between 1 and 2 inches. South of Fresno County, the valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rain. Between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall over much of the Kern County Deserts. The snow level was around 7000 feet for much of the event, but lowered to around 5500 feet during the morning of February 14 following the passage of the main cold front. Several SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 2 to 3 feet of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Lebec Plateau Court measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper ridge built inland into California during the morning of February 26. With clear skies, light winds and inversion conditions present during the morning hours, an area of dense fog developed in the central portion of the San Joaquin Valley which impacted travel along the SR 198 corridor and along portions of SR 99 in Tulare County during the morning commute. There were also patches of short lived dense fog elsewhere in the San Joaquin Valley. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck not long after sunrise and completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of one eighth of a mile was reported in Hanford. Webcams indicated visibility below 1000 feet in Hanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain with river and roadway flooding, and gusty southerly winds February 3rd through 5th. Multiple feet of heavy snow were observed above 5000 feet, with 2 to 5 inches of rain observed in the Valley and foothills and southerly wind gusts of up to 50 mph. There were numerous reports of flooding in the Valley that led to multiple search and rescue efforts and prolonged road closures. Portions of Highway 99 were closed due to flooding, State Route 70 was closed from Greenville Wye to Jarbo Gap due to a slide, and Interstate 80 was closed in both directions for about 5 hours from Nyack to Truckee due to crashes and icy roads. There were also reports of numerous downed branches and trees across the area with associated power outages. Glenn County reported over 20 road closures across their area due to flooding and storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sutter County Office of Emergency Management reported that Placer Road was closed between Riosa Road and Brewer Road due to flooding from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm system brought another round of heavy snow, rain, rock slides and gusty winds from February 6th through February 7th. Generally 4 to 12 inches were observed above 3000 feet in Shasta County and the Coast Range and above 5000 feet in the Sierra and Southern Cascades, with up to 18 inches at the higher peaks. Interstate 5 closed for about 4 hours at Pollard Flat due to heavy snow. Traffic cameras showed light snowfall reported down to 800 feet in Shasta County as well. State Route 70 closed from Jarbo Gap to Greenville Wye due to a significant rock slide. California Highway Patrol reported multiple cars stuck in snow in the mountains and a few downed trees in the Valley and foothills. There were multiple reports of roadway flooding in the Valley and foothills, including the closure of Highway 99 between Garner Lane and Keefer Road due to floodwaters covering the roadway. The Sacramento River experienced flooding due to heavy rain and high flows that led to the flooding of two RV Parks within the levee along the river in Colusa and Sutter Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colusa Landing RV Park along the Sacramento River within the levee flooded due to heavy rain and river flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong winter storm on February 13 and 14 brought enough precipitation to the area to offset the drought conditions which had developed in much of Kern County due to a long term precipitation deficit. The drought ended over the Tehachapi Mountains, but moderate drought conditions persisted in the desert areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Valley was classified as being in an moderate drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure systems transitioned through SOCAL during the week from the 4th thru 9th of May, bringing periods of moderate to heavy rain, strong and gusty winds, and snow to the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet reported maximum gust of 48 mph at 10:08 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 12th, a weather system brought a brief period of moderate snow showers that led to multiple spinouts and a jackknifed big rig. These accidents resulted in the closure of I-80 westbound at Truckee. Traffic was initially stopped at 4:46 PM PDT but was then closed from 4:56 PM PDT through 7:54 PM PDT. Chain restrictions were in effect for trucks for most of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather system brought a brief period of moderate snow showers that led to multiple spinouts and a jackknifed big rig. These accidents resulted in the closure of I-80 westbound at Truckee. Traffic was initially stopped at 4:46 PM PDT but was then closed from 4:56 PM PDT through 7:54 PM PDT. Chain restrictions were in effect for trucks for most of the day. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 5.9 inches of snow in 24 hours at 6894 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two cold water drownings were discovered in the month of May due to area waterways running fast and cold from snowmelt. One occurred on the American River near Oregon Bar. Another occurred on the Sacramento River and was a 23 year old fisherman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Placer County Sheriff Office dive team discovered a body in the American River Sunday, May 18th, 2025 around 3 PM PDT in the Oregon Bar area of the river. Water temperatures at the time of body discovery were in the low 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two cold water drownings were discovered in the month of May due to area waterways running fast and cold from snowmelt. One occurred on the American River near Oregon Bar. Another occurred on the Sacramento River and was a 23 year old fisherman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media shared information that crews found the body of a missing 23 year old fisherman who went into the water to cool down. He was walking in the river at waist level when he hit a drop off and was pulled underwater and struggled to swim. He went under the surface and never came back up. He was not wearing a life jacket. Water temperatures at the time were estimated to be between 65 and 70 degrees. The drowning occurred in Yolo County on the Sacramento River near Old River Road and Levee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms formed mainly over Mono, southern Lyon and southern Mineral counties with several hundred recorded lightning strikes and strong outflow winds during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak outflow gust at Mill Canyon RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense atmospheric river impacted the Bay Area from February 3rd to the 4th, bringing heavy rain and strong wind to the region, especially across the North Bay. The Russian River near Guerneville flooded due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house in the 12000 block of Westside Road in Forestville fell into the Russian River around 1:30 PM on February 4th. Debris made its way to the Pacific Ocean.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Russian River at Johnson's Beach in Guerneville crested at 35.6 feet during the day on February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm on February 13 caused widespread flooding and strong winds across the Bay Area and Central Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The number 1 lane was partially flooded on US Highway 101 southbound just south of Arroyo Seco Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm on February 13 caused widespread flooding and strong winds across the Bay Area and Central Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harrison Road north of Salinas was closed between Sala Road to Russell Road because of reported flooding on Harrison and Sala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another winter storm moved through on March 14th bringing additional heavy mountain snow down into the portions of the foothills, rain and gusty winds to interior northern California. A dusting of snow up to an inch was measured down to Redding at 681 feet in the northern Sacramento Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby CoCoRaHS at similar elevations measured 2 to 4 inches of snow above 2000 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 1 to 6 inches of snow above 2000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From March 11th through 13th a winter storm brought heavy mountain snow, strong southerly winds and moderate to heavy rain to interior northern California. Snow amounts of 1 to over 3 feet were observed in the mountains. Roadway flooding was observed in the Valley and foothills, with downed trees and powerlines from strong southerly winds. On March 12th a line of non-severe storms moved through portions of the Sacramento Valley producing wind gusts over 50 mph. This resulted in a large oak tree to fall and damage an apartment building in Sacramento County. As the line of storms moved through the foothills, numerous trees and powerlines came down leading to power outages in the area. Traffic was being turned on Interstate 80 at Alta for a period due to spinouts and heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby CoCoRaHS at similar elevations measured 1 to 4 inches of snow above 3000 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 1 to 6 inches of snow above 3000 feet with 6 to 18 inches at the higher peaks over the period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large metal fence had fallen over the southbound lanes on Bell Road in Placer County. Auburn Municipal Airport measured a max gust of 44 mph on March 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of significant winter storms brought heavy mountain snow, moderate to heavy rain and strong southerly winds to interior northern California from March 15th through March 17th. Numerous down trees and powerlines were reported, especially up in Shasta and Tehama counties. Thunderstorms brought hail up to 1 inch in size in the mountains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent late season system moved through the region from the 11th to 12th. This produced widespread strong, damaging winds throughout western Nevada and the eastern Sierra. This storm additionally produced a period of accumulating snowfall along Interstate 80 over Donner Pass, leading to a temporary closure during the afternoon of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season winter storm brought numerous weather hazards to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals generally ranged between 0.50 and 1.50 inches across coastal areas up to 4 inches in the mountains. Winter storm conditions developed in the mountains with snowfall totals up to 18 inches and southerly wind gusts up to 77 MPH.  Thunderstorms, associated with this system, brought some reports of flooding as well as a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the northern mountains of Ventura county. Some snowfall totals include: Mount Pinos (6-12 inches), Pine Mountain Club (4-8 inches) and Cuddy Valley (4-8 inches). Along with the snowfall, strong southerly winds, gusting up to 50 MPH, were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong high pressure system developed across the region. As a result, a warming trend began early on August 5th with temperatures in the upper 90s to lower 100s. Warming continued for the next several days as high pressure strengthened over our forecast area. Highs reached around 105 degrees for at least a few consecutive days, or until the 11th, in the Central Valley and over 110 degrees in the Kern County desert, with the hottest temperatures in the Indian Wells Valley. A slight lowering trend began on the 12th, but highs remained at least a few degrees above seasonal averages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 105 and 110 degrees each day between August 9 and August 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough slowly moved through California between the evening of March 30 and the early morning of April 2. This system brough widespread precipitation to the area between the early morning of March 31 and the evening of April 1. Much of the Sierra  Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between three quarters on an inch to 2 inches of precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picking up an estimated 10 to 20 inches of snowfall during the extent of the event. The snow level lowered from around 6000 feet on the onset of the event lowering to near 4500 feet by the evening of April 1. Meanwhile, much of the central San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while the south valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch. The Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter to a half inch of precipitation while the deserts were rain shadowed and picked up a tenth of an inch or less. Strong wind gusts also accompanied this system and several station in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured peak wind gusts between 50 and 70 mph on March 31 and April 1 before wind diminished on April 2 as the trough moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interaction between a deepening upper trough over the Great Basin region and an amplifying upper ridge off the California coast produced a strong northerly flow over central California. The wind mixed down during the afternoon and evening of April 17 resulting in a period of strong wind gusts across portions of the mountains and deserts of Kern County. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph with a few stations briefly recording gusts near 60 mph. The winds decreased during the early morning of April 18 as the trough shifted slightly eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring storm brought heavy mountain snow, rain, gusty winds and thunderstorms to interior northern California at the end of March and carried into the evening of April 1st. Storm total snow of 24 to 44 inches was reported above 5000 feet, from March 31 to April 2. Traffic was held on Interstate 80 between Alta and NV stateline due to heavy snow from approximately 10 AM to 2 PM on April 1st, with US Highway 50 held from Meyers to Echo Summit from 12 PM through 3 PM on April 1st. An EF-0 tornado was confirmed in Stanislaus County as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado touched down at 2 ENE Salida at 2:13 PM PDT and was on the ground for 0.9 miles until 2:15 PM PDT. The tornado moved through several orchards and 3 properties. Dozens of almond trees were uprooted and large tree branches were broken. Minor structure damage observed at the properties included a covered patio and roof material ripped off, a broken window, and outbuilding and metal fencing was displaced, and a large trailer was lifted and moved approximately 20 feet.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system approached the Central California coast on April 25 which spread brought increased cloud cover and cooler temperatures across the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra Nevada with a light few showers being observed over the Sierra Nevada. The low moved into and across central California on April 26 bringing rain, higher elevation snow and increased winds to the desert areas. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall although a few locations which were impacted by thunderstorms picked up more. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of liquid precipitation with much of it in the form of snow above 5000 feet with many SNOTELS above 6000 feet picking up between 2 and 5 inches of new snowfall. Yosemite Park picked up a bit more snowfall with an estimated 3 to 8 inches indicated by SNOTELS. This system also produced wind gusts between 40 and 55 mph across the Kern County Desert areas and some thunderstorms with pea sized hail in the San Joaquin Valley and lower Sierra Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picked up an estimated 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans District 3 measured 8 to 14 inches of snow above 5000 feet in 24 hours. UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab measured 13.2 inches over the same timeframe. Ski resorts in the Sierra measured 7 to 18 inches of snow above 6500 feet. The National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center estimated 24 hour snow totals to be around 2 to 6 inches above 3500 feet with 6 to 12 inches above 5000 feet and up to 12 to 18 inches at the highest peaks. Traffic was held on Interstate 80 between Alta and NV stateline due to heavy snow from approximately 10 AM to 2 PM on April 1st, with US Highway 50 held from Meyers to Echo Summit from 12 PM through 3 PM on April 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A water rescue operation occurred in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of Davenport on April 7. First responders were called to Davenport Landing at 2:17 PM as four people were in the water and struggling to return to shore. Three rescue swimmers from Cal Fire and two from California State Parks jumped in and brought all four people to shore unharmed. At the time of the report, observations from a Saildrone off Half Moon Bay and the Point Reyes buoy reported waves around 10 feet and a dominant period of 13 to 14 seconds. Data from the Point Reyes buoy suggests that a 9 foot west-northwest swell at 13 seconds dominated the wave spectrum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue operation occurred in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of Davenport on April 7. First responders were called to Davenport Landing at 2:17 PM as four people were in the water and struggling to return to shore. Three rescue swimmers from Cal Fire and two from California State Parks jumped in and brought all four people to shore unharmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder low pressure moved slowly across the region from the 25th-27th, dropping daytime temperatures to 10-15 degrees below average. Patchy light rain showers developed across much of the region on the 25th, with isolated thunderstorms over west central NV. More widespread rain and snow developed during the late night of the 25th into the 26th, and again the following night, bringing late season snow for the Sierra and a rain-snow mix down to lower elevations of western NV. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2-7��� for the Tahoe basin and into northern Mono County, while in western NV up to 2��� of snow fell (mainly on unpaved surfaces) around the main cities of far western NV, and up to 4��� in Virginia City. An additional 1-5��� of snow fell in the Tahoe basin with up to 8��� in higher elevations during the late night of the 26th into the morning of the 27th (Photos 4 to 6). Rainfall was heaviest across far northeast CA and northwest NV, with several sites receiving between 0.50 and 1 inch, and up to 1.25 inch in parts of the Surprise Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs station CA-MN-8 Walker 1.2 NE, Cocorahs station CA-MN-5 Mammoth Lakes 16.3 ESE, and Cocorahs station CA-MN-6 Mono City 1.9 SW reported 7.2, 3.5, and 1.2 inches of snowfall, respectively.

Patchy light rain showers developed across much of the region on the 25th, with isolated thunderstorms over west central NV. More widespread rain and snow developed during the late night of the 25th into the 26th, and again the following night, bringing late season snow for the Sierra and a rain-snow mix down to lower elevations of western NV. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2-7��� for the Tahoe basin and into northern Mono County, while in western NV up to 2��� of snow fell (mainly on unpaved surfaces) around the main cities of far western NV, and up to 4��� in Virginia City. An additional 1-5��� of snow fell in the Tahoe basin with up to 8��� in higher elevations during the late night of the 26th into the morning of the 27th (Photos 4 to 6). Rainfall was heaviest across far northeast CA and northwest NV, with several sites receiving between 0.50 and 1 inch, and up to 1.25 inch in parts of the Surprise Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer in Tahoma, TAAC1, and a CO-OP observer at Boca Reservoir, BOCC1, and a CO-OP observer in Tahoe City, TAHC1, reported a 2-day storm total of 2.6, 6.5, and 4.5 inches of snow, respectively. The Central Sierra Snow Lab reported a 2-day storm total of 8.8 inches with 9 inches at Palisades Tahoe Ski area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough slowly moved through California between the evening of March 30 and the early morning of April 2. This system brough widespread precipitation to the area between the early morning of March 31 and the evening of April 1. Much of the Sierra  Nevada and adjacent foothills picked up between three quarters on an inch to 2 inches of precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTEL stations picking up an estimated 10 to 20 inches of snowfall during the extent of the event. The snow level lowered from around 6000 feet on the onset of the event lowering to near 4500 feet by the evening of April 1. Meanwhile, much of the central San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall while the south valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch. The Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter to a half inch of precipitation while the deserts were rain shadowed and picked up a tenth of an inch or less. Strong wind gusts also accompanied this system and several station in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured peak wind gusts between 50 and 70 mph on March 31 and April 1 before wind diminished on April 2 as the trough moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL stations picked up an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that moved in from the eastern Pacific combined with abundant low-level moisture with precipitable water values exceeding 1, which is near record levels for early June, to result in pockets of showers and thunderstorms across portions of southeast CA during the afternoon and evening hours on the 3rd. An area of heavy rainfall developed along west-central Imperial County along Fish Creek Wash, resulting in amounts exceeding 1 in less than an hour, leading to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure impacted the area from June 19th through the 21st which brought significant winds for late June. This was combined with very low humidity which lead to several days of fire weather concerns. Some light rain and snow was recorded in far northern Washoe and Lassen counties the morning of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy data on Lake Tahoe indicated a northerly wind gust of 45 mph from nearby showers and collapsing thunderstorms. This caused significant waves on Lake Tahoe, with multiple boats damaged and one boat capsized near Emerald Bay, causing 8 fatalities out of the 10 on board. The two that survived were wearing life jackets, while the other 8 did not.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river and stationary front brought heavy snow and gusty winds with the most significant impacts on 02/03 (Monday morning). The snowfall accumulation was high across the area, which was unusually high for areas east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For all but the highest elevations in the zone, downslope flow and relatively warm air limited snowfall during the majority of the episode.  This changed during the latter portion. The National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 6 to 12 inches at elevations between 3000 and 5000 feet, with amounts of 4 to 8 inches down to valley floors.  Precipitation amounts in the zone included 2.75 inches at Montague, 3.31 inches at Montague-Siskiyou Airport, and 1.68 inches at Weed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plane crash into the Pacific Ocean near Pacific Grove on July 26 killed all three people aboard. A twin-engine Beechcraft, tail number N8796R, departed from San Carlos Airport in the Bay Area at approximately 10:07 p.m., heading to Monterey Regional Airport. However, around 10:38 p.m., the aircraft crashed into the ocean 200-300 yards off Point Pinos near Sunset Drive and Jewell Avenue. At the time of the incident, Monterey Regional Airport reported IFR conditions with ceilings at 800 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A twin-engine Beechcraft, tail number N8796R, departed from San Carlos Airport in the Bay Area at approximately 10:07 p.m., heading to Monterey Regional Airport. However, around 10:38 p.m., the aircraft crashed into the ocean 200-300 yards off Point Pinos near Sunset Drive and Jewell Avenue. At the time of the incident, Monterey Regional Airport reported IFR conditions with ceilings at 800 feet. Rescuers found one of the victims dead in the water, with aircraft debris washing up ashore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A magnitude 8.8 earthquake occurred off the Kamchatka Peninsula in eastern Russia on the 29th of July, Pacific Time. The tsunami wave then traveled across the Pacific and impacted the Northwest California coast around midnight on the 30th. Tsunami waves continue to impact the coast through the 30th into the 31st at levels exceeding warning and advisory levels in Crescent City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tsunami waves impacted the Crescent City Harbor beginning around midnight on the 30th of July. The waves reached peak amplitude quickly in the first few hours just under 4 feet in amplitude. The waves then continued for over 24 hours, finally falling below the 1 foot tsunami advisory threshold during the morning of the 31st. The waves severely damaged or destroyed H-Dock in the harbor and damaged many of the connections between the other docks. The initial damage estimate is around $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pickett Fire broke out to the north of Calistoga on August 21 around 3 PM, and would eventually burn 6803 acres, leading to evacuations across mainly rural areas of Napa County, including the community of Aetna Springs. Hot and dry conditions, with warm overnight lows and poor overnight humidity recoveries in the higher elevations, presented elevated fire weather threats across the interior through the first few days of the fire's lifecycle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a mix of wet and dry thunderstorms in the area led to the ignition of the Green Fire in Shasta Trinity National Forest on July 1st, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a mix of wet and dry thunderstorms in the area led to the ignition of the Green Fire in Shasta Trinity National Forest on July 1st, 2025. A period of very hot and dry weather followed that allowed for the fire to spread. Periodically breezy diurnal winds also aided in the spread of the fire. As of July 31st, the fire sits at a 92% containment and 19,022 acres. Evacuation warnings were issued in the surrounding areas as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving through N CA/NV provided plentiful forcing for storms to develop on July 30. In fact, showers lingered overnight the night before across portions of the Sierra and NE CA. These storms produced robust outflows with multiple areas of blowing dust and one true haboob/dust storm. We also had flooding that closed a roadway that afternoon/evening along US-395 near the Red Rock area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall lead to mudslides and rockslides along US-395 north of Red Rock Rd. This caused both lanes of traffic to shutdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment across southeast California on August 25th was primed for strong to severe thunderstorms with very moist and unstable conditions in place. The synoptic pattern featured a subtropical ridge of high pressure centered over northern New Mexico, while a Pacific trough to the west promoted upper level divergence over the region. These synoptic features also allowed for southerly moisture advection with PWATs climbing upwards of 1.8-2.0 inches. The broad ascent over the region combined with abundant moisture, MLCAPE values in excess of 1,000 to 2,000 J/kg, and DCAPE values in excess of 1,000 J/kg provided an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorms capable of producing damaging winds and flash flooding. Daytime heating allowed for continued destabilization with thunderstorms initiating midday across portions of Imperial County near the international border. These thunderstorms became strong to severe through the afternoon, producing numerous instances of wind damage as well as flash flooding, primarily across the Imperial Valley. The most significant flash flooding occurred along Highway 86 near Highway 78 when approximately 200 motorists became stranded in floodwaters. After being hit by damaging thunderstorms on both August 24th and 25th, the Imperial Irrigation District (IID) reported 311 downed power poles with damage estimates for the utility company upwards of 9 to 12 million dollars. Several schools across the Imperial Valley were closed on the 26th after sustaining damage from this event. No fatalities were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans reported downed power lines blocking both directions of I-8 near the Gordons Well Road exit. No injuries were reported. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet sites in the northern Owens Valley topped 60 mph, with a peak of 67 mph at Pine Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The subtropical high pressure was situated near the Four Corners Region with south to southeasterly flow in place across much of southern CA. This southerly flow pulled in abundant moisture with Precipitable Water Values exceeding 1.5. The combination of the moisture and daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE in excess of 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG. Widespread thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon across the San Diego Mountains and the northern Baja chain with an isolated severe thunderstorm erupting over southern portions of the Imperial Valley just southwest of El Centro. This thunderstorm was nearly stationary producing damaging winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility under 1/4 mile just outside the city of Imperial due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A four corners high pressure advected a high amount of atmospheric moisture into the region. This was day one of an extended push of monsoon moisture that produced heavy rain, localized flooding, strong outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 0.33 inches of rain in 20 minutes as well as pea size hail. In addition, there was a 51 mph thunderstorm wind gust measure at JPL04 mesonet station on Lake Tahoe.

The combination of the moisture and daytime heating resulted in MLCAPE in excess of 1000 J/KG with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/KG. Widespread thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon across the San Diego Mountains and the northern Baja chain with an isolated severe thunderstorm erupting over southern portions of the Imperial Valley just southwest of El Centro. This thunderstorm was nearly stationary producing damaging winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power poles along Schaniel Road near the intersection with Harrigan Road. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. Timing estimated from radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment across southeast California on August 25th was primed for strong to severe thunderstorms with very moist and unstable conditions in place. The synoptic pattern featured a subtropical ridge of high pressure centered over northern New Mexico, while a Pacific trough to the west promoted upper level divergence over the region. These synoptic features also allowed for southerly moisture advection with PWATs climbing upwards of 1.8-2.0 inches. The broad ascent over the region combined with abundant moisture, MLCAPE values in excess of 1,000 to 2,000 J/kg, and DCAPE values in excess of 1,000 J/kg provided an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorms capable of producing damaging winds and flash flooding. Daytime heating allowed for continued destabilization with thunderstorms initiating midday across portions of Imperial County near the international border. These thunderstorms became strong to severe through the afternoon, producing numerous instances of wind damage as well as flash flooding, primarily across the Imperial Valley. The most significant flash flooding occurred along Highway 86 near Highway 78 when approximately 200 motorists became stranded in floodwaters. After being hit by damaging thunderstorms on both August 24th and 25th, the Imperial Irrigation District (IID) reported 311 downed power poles with damage estimates for the utility company upwards of 9 to 12 million dollars. Several schools across the Imperial Valley were closed on the 26th after sustaining damage from this event. No fatalities were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four semi trucks were reported to be flipped over with power lines over the trucks. Images also showed multiple power poles down in the area. No injuries were reported. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level high pressure over the southwest US produced well above average temperatures for several days during the first half of August. Most inland areas saw dangerous heat levels with the low elevations of the deserts being particularly affected. Temperatures peaked on August 7th, a day that produced several record highs across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily record high temperature of 104 at Ramona Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low off the west coast sent two cold fronts across northern California with gusty southerly winds. Snow levels were high, at around 5500 feet, with the first front from 12/24 . It brought moderate snow. Heavy snow then occurred at elevations above 3000 feet with the slower, colder, stronger second front on 12/25 and  post-frontal snow showers on 12/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 8 to 20 inches at elevations  above 3000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.14 inches at Etna, 1.39 inches at Sawyers Bar, 1.84 inches at Callahan #2, and 0.86 inches at Quartz Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 10 to 30 inches at elevations above 3000 feet, except up to 48 inches over the high terrain, including Mt. Shasta. Heavy snow initially fell at elevations above 5500 feet, but impacts increased with falling snow levels during the day on 12/25.  Precipitation amounts in the zone included 6.05 inches at Mt. Shasta City, 4.35 inches at Ash Creek, and 4.49 inches at Upton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A notable early season rainstorm caused minor flooding and thunderstorm impacts across the Bay Area and Central Coast on October 13th and 14th. Many parts of the region saw the most rainfall since the last winter season, and some areas received the equivalent of an entire October's worth of rainfall. Towards the evening of the 13th and the 14th, thunderstorms started to impact the Central Coast. A cell with strong straight line winds damaged a farm near Pescadero in southern San Mateo County, and two funnel clouds were reported in Salinas and Hollister.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm cell touched down just south of Ano Nuevo State Park at 4:23 PM. Video from a farm near the location showed damage to structures, sparking speculation of a possible tornado. Subsequently, an NWS team conducted a storm survey at the site. The team identified damage consistent with straight line winds. Damage was noted from the coast, just south of A��o Nuevo State Park, eastward about 3/4 of a mile. This damage included, but not limited to, a hoop house visqueen cover being torn off, structural damage to two hoop houses, a large water storage tank being transported over a 1/4 mile, and moderately large diameter coastal cypress trees being snapped. There was one item seen transported counter to straight line wind flow and these were determined to have been caught up in very small scale temporary terrain driven  rotation. There is no indication in the damage field of tornadic activity. While the radar signature did show rotation at elevation while approaching the coast, it appears to collapse as it comes  ashore, lending itself to thunderstorm outflow and a straight line wind event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure, tapped into an atmospheric river driving up subtropical moisture, produced substantial rainfall across southern California. This led to some areas within the San Bernardino mountains receiving well over a foot of total rain on Christmas Eve, and resulted in significant flooding for the city of Wrightwood. There also road closures due to debris flow along Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Baldy Road severely impacted and closed in places due to flooding and road washouts. Mt. Baldy Rd at Shin Rd was closed due to multiple land and mudslides. Stations in the  area recorded 5.75-6.5 inches of rain in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Glen Helen Parkway and a nearby construction site near the entrance of Cajon pass in northern Rialto had been overwhelmed with floodwaters. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of atmospheric rivers brought moderate to heavy precipitation to interior northern California in the third week of December through the Christmas holiday. A Convergence Zone set up over Redding/Shasta County around 1 PM on Sunday, December 21. Heavy rain fell until around 7 PM, where there was a lull before another line redeveloped and brought additional moderate to heavy rain into the late evening. Over 24 hours, the area received 2 to 7 inches of rain. There were numerous reports of flash flooding, water damage, vehicles getting stuck in the rising waters, and one fatality. There was another Swiftwater rescue that occurred at 1:15 PM at Cisco Grove near Donner Summit as water levels rose from heavy rainfall on the South Yuba River. 4-6 inches of rain were observed over the previous 24 hours in this area. Numerous down trees and power lines were also reported from gusty southerly winds of 35 to 55 mph across the northern/central Sacramento valley and Sierra foothills. Several mud and rock slides were also reported in the foothills. Snow levels were high with this round, through December 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding from heavy rain with the river coming over the bridge at State Route 99 and Old Ranch Road. A semi truck and 6 to 7 vehicles were stuck as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very wet period from December 19th-21st, another round of storms brought more rain and damaging winds to the region in the days leading up to Christmas. Impacts were felt across the region from December 23rd to the 26th. The worst of the winds impacts Mendocino County where most of the coast lost power, some areas for more than 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems impacted the Bay Area and Central Coast from December 19 to 25th, impacting Christmas holiday travel across the region. The most impactful systems arrived on Christmas Eve and Christmas Day. Two tornado warnings were issued for the coastal regions of San Mateo and Santa Cruz counties based off radar indicated rotation. San Francisco International Airport reported delays of at least 300 flights during the storms, and over 400,000 customers across the Bay Area and 30,000 in the Central Coast lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge along Belmont Creek at the Harbor Boulevard and Old County Road intersection reported major flooding, up to a stage of 5.64 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft with an inversion layer persisted since the last week of November. Low temperatures dipped into the mid 30s at times when the low clouds cleared in the Central Valley. Otherwise, low temperatures were only slightly cooler when the low clouds, rather than fog, lingered. Patchy dense fog occurred at times during the first five days of the month in the San Joaquin Valley, but lifted to a stratus deck for the next few days. Dense fog was prevalent in the foothill areas, including the Grapevine, the Sierra Nevada foothills, and the coastal ranges at elevations between 1,000 feet and 2,500 feet from the 1st until the 9th.||Elsewhere, seasonably cool temperatures were recorded while dry conditions were present, such as in the mountains above 2,500 feet and the Kern County desert. Freezing overnight low temperatures were reported in the desert during the first several days of the month, which is common during December.||Fog and low stratus cloud cover continued over the San Joaquin Valley, as well as the lower Sierra Nevada foothills and coastal ranges up to around 2,500 feet for much of the second week of the month. However, dense fog became most prevalent in the San Joaquin Valley each night and morning from the 12th until the 15th. Most locations in the valley observed dense fog, even when they did not observe any clearing during the daytime on the 12th until the 15th. Enough moisture remained trapped in the Central Valley while the inversion layer served as a lid over the mountains and foothills due to the stagnant high pressure pattern in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reports dense fog on the Grapevine portion of I-5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river moved through California late on December 23rd into the afternoon of December 24th. Very heavy rainfall and strong gusty winds were observed across most of our County Warning Area (CWA). The heaviest rainfall was observed in Fresno, Mariposa and Kern County. Rainfall ranged from around half inch up to four inches in some localized areas. Flash flooding occurred mainly in eastern Kern County were several roads or closed.||Wet pattern began in the region during the early morning hours of the 24th and lasted until the 26th. Many locations reported 2-4 inches of rain, and some other locations reported as much as 9-10 inches of rain, mainly towards Shaver Lake and Dinkey Creek in the Sierra Nevada in Fresno County. About 2-4 feet of snow fell at elevations above 7,000 feet, and snow levels lowered to about 6,000 feet by the 26th. Some vehicles were stuck in snow as far south as Kern County, or in Pine Mountain Club on the afternoon of the 26th. Heavy rain prompted Flood Warnings over the burn scars in Fresno and Kern Counties and Flood Advisories in the Central Valley and quite a bit of the higher terrain adjacent to the San Joaquin Valley. Gusty winds developed each day during this period, with the strongest gusts in the Coastal Ranges that reached above 60 mph. In addition, isolated thunderstorms popped up over parts of the Central Valley in the afternoon and early evening of the 26th which produced mainly lightning, gusty winds, and brief moderate rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reports flooding on AVENUE 128 / ROAD 184.

Impacts were felt across the region from December 23rd to the 26th. The worst of the winds impacts Mendocino County where most of the coast lost power, some areas for more than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: RAWS station near Yorkville reported a wind gust to 78 mph. Additional gusts between 40 and 55 mph were reported in the area. Multiple trees were downed and power outages were widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of wet weather brought heavy snowfall and strong winds to the Sierra Nevada. Heaviest snowfall was observed from 12/24-12/26 when snow levels came down below the mountain passes. Snowfall measurements over the 12/24-12/26 timeframe were around 2-5 ft per Caltrans, Ski resorts, and UC Berkeley Central Sierra Snow Lab. Southerly wind gusts of 50-80 mph were observed as well across the Valley, foothills and mountains that resulted in numerous downed trees and thousands of power outages. There were also numerous reports of flooding in the lower elevations from heavy rain, including two fatalities and 3 injuries from vehicles that hydroplaned and crashed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the canal flooded the bridge on Kirksville Road, making it impassable. One person drove their car into the high waters and became stranded, requiring a water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of low pressure, tapped into an atmospheric river driving up subtropical moisture, produced substantial rainfall across southern California. This led to some areas within the San Bernardino mountains receiving well over a foot of total rain on Christmas Eve, and resulted in significant flooding for the city of Wrightwood. There also road closures due to debris flow along Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways reported excessive mud and debris running onto north and southbound I-15, where I-15 intersects Hwy 138 through the Cajon Pass. Portions of I-15 had to be closed temporarily through the Cajon Pass as crews worked to clear the mud/debris.

Impacts were felt across the region from December 23rd to the 26th. The worst of the winds impacts Mendocino County where most of the coast lost power, some areas for more than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a very large tree down across Highway 299 blocking both lanes of the highway near Bair Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong atmospheric river moved through California late on December 23rd into the afternoon of December 24th. Very heavy rainfall and strong gusty winds were observed across most of our County Warning Area (CWA). The heaviest rainfall was observed in Fresno, Mariposa and Kern County. Rainfall ranged from around half inch up to four inches in some localized areas. Flash flooding occurred mainly in eastern Kern County were several roads or closed.||Wet pattern began in the region during the early morning hours of the 24th and lasted until the 26th. Many locations reported 2-4 inches of rain, and some other locations reported as much as 9-10 inches of rain, mainly towards Shaver Lake and Dinkey Creek in the Sierra Nevada in Fresno County. About 2-4 feet of snow fell at elevations above 7,000 feet, and snow levels lowered to about 6,000 feet by the 26th. Some vehicles were stuck in snow as far south as Kern County, or in Pine Mountain Club on the afternoon of the 26th. Heavy rain prompted Flood Warnings over the burn scars in Fresno and Kern Counties and Flood Advisories in the Central Valley and quite a bit of the higher terrain adjacent to the San Joaquin Valley. Gusty winds developed each day during this period, with the strongest gusts in the Coastal Ranges that reached above 60 mph. In addition, isolated thunderstorms popped up over parts of the Central Valley in the afternoon and early evening of the 26th which produced mainly lightning, gusty winds, and brief moderate rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported flooding on SR 137 between Rd 46 and Road 48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems impacted the Bay Area and Central Coast from December 19 to 25th, impacting Christmas holiday travel across the region. The most impactful systems arrived on Christmas Eve and Christmas Day. Two tornado warnings were issued for the coastal regions of San Mateo and Santa Cruz counties based off radar indicated rotation. San Francisco International Airport reported delays of at least 300 flights during the storms, and over 400,000 customers across the Bay Area and 30,000 in the Central Coast lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station on North Peak Access Road reported a gust of 67 mph.
